concept:
Tested 5 2006/03/09 23:02 Once a patients signs a statement giving consent, they must continue treatment. False. Patients may change their minds at any time. True. The signed statement is a contract which may not be broken by either party.
Exceptions to the requirement of informed consent
Emergency situations
Patients without decision-making capacity
Patients who sign waivers to the rights of informed consent
A 15 year-old pregnant girl requires hospitalization for preeclampsia. Should her parents be informed? No. Parental consent is not necessary for medical treatment of pregnant minors. Yes. Minors do not have the capacity to consent so they're legal guardians must consent to any medical treatment if it is not an emergency.
What problems arise if a doctor refers a patient for an MRI at a facility he owns. Conflict of interest
Involuntary psychiatric hospitalization can be undertaken for which three reasons?
Patient is a danger to self
Patient is a danger to others
Patient is gravely disabled (unable to provide for basic needs)
Withdrawing life-sustaining care is a more serious measure than withholding care. False. Withdrawing and withholding life-sustaining care are the same from an ethical standpoint. True. Withdrawing life-sustaining care is an active act whereas withholding life-sustaining care is in-action and falls into a lesser category of liability.
When can a physician refuse to continue treating a patient on grounds of futility?
When there is no rationale for treatment
Maximal intervention is failing
A given intervention has already failed
Treatment will not achive the goals of care
An 8 year-old child is in a serious accident. She requires emergent transfusion but her parents are not present. Treat immediately. Consent is implied in emergency situations. Wait until the legal guardians can be found.
Conditions in which confidentiality must be overriden.
Real threat of harm to third parties
Suicidality
Certain contagious diseases (e.g. AIDS)
Elder and child abuse
Involuntary commitment or isolation for medical treatment may be undertaken for what reason? Treatment noncompliance represents a serious threat to public health (e.g. active TB)
A 10 year-old child presents in status epilepticus, but her parents refuse treatment on religious grounds. Treat because the disease represents an immediate threat to the child's life. Then seek a court order. The parents are the legal guardians of the patient and have the right to refuse treatment on the basis of their religion.
A son asks that his mother not be told about her recently discovered cancer. A patient's family cannot require that a doctor withhold information from the patient. The patient's family can withhold information from the patient when the doctor deems that there is no medical benefit to informing the patient.
